steps.title: Find the third angle in triangle QXS
steps.content:
In triangle QXS, we have two angles: ∠SQX = 48° and ∠SXQ = 86°. Using the angle sum property:
$$∠QSX = 180° - 48° - 86° = 46°$$
This is the angle at vertex S between ray SQ (along line PV) and ray SX (toward point X). Since W lies on segment SX, this same angle applies to ray SW.

steps.title: Use vertical angles at W to find ∠RWS
steps.content:
At point W, lines RU and SX intersect. We're given ∠SWU = 85°.

Since R, W, and U are collinear on line RU, rays WR and WU point in opposite directions. Therefore:
$$∠RWS + ∠SWU = 180°$$
$$∠RWS = 180° - 85° = 95°$$

steps.title: Find ∠TRU using triangle RWS
steps.content:
In triangle RWS, we now have:
• ∠RWS = 95°
• ∠WSR = 46° (same as ∠QSX, since both measure from ray along PV to ray SX)

Therefore:
$$∠WRS = 180° - 95° - 46° = 39°$$
Since W lies between R and U on line RU, ray RW and ray RU point in the same direction, so ∠URS = ∠WRS = 39°. Since both S and T lie to the right of R on line PV, rays RS and RT are the same ray, giving us ∠TRU = 39°.

steps.title: Find ∠RTU and solve for ∠TUR
steps.content:
At point T on line PV, we're given ∠VTU = 162°. Since P and V lie on opposite sides of T along line PV:
$$∠PTU = 180° - 162° = 18°$$
Since R lies between P and T on line PV, ray TR points in the same direction as ray TP, so ∠RTU = 18°.

Now in triangle RTU:
$$∠RTU + ∠TRU + ∠TUR = 180°$$
$$18° + 39° + ∠TUR = 180°$$
$$∠TUR = 123°$$
The answer is 123 degrees.
